summary: Ran cambium's first real end-to-end knowledge cycle on balatron history (distill -> recall -> promote to team -> endorse -> org PR -> cross-project recall). Federation validated: balatron-earned knowledge recalled from the Clark project.
problem: Cambium had never been exercised on a real project's history end to end. We needed to prove the local->team->org promotion lifecycle and, critically, cross-project recall actually work before relying on cambium as the org knowledge layer.
why_chosen: Drove cambium_server.py functions directly (the cambium MCP tools were not exposed in the session) with CAMBIUM_REPO=balatron, CAMBIUM_ORG_REPO=knowledge, org PR mode on. distill() produced 80 items (62 decisions + 18 constraints) entirely from context-keeper; agentsync contributed 0 (see companion constraint). Five standing recall queries, run to cross the recalls>=3 gate, promoted 22 items to the team cambium branch via the worktree+CAS path. con-016 (doc-sync) and con-014 (regime-boundary) were endorsed as universal practice and promoted to org as PRs #1/#2 (since merged). Pointing cambium at the Clark repo with the same org store, both items recalled back stamped project=balatron — the pass condition for compound, cross-project knowledge growth.
what_we_tried: A single pass of the five queries maxed at 2 recalls, so the queries were repeated across 3 rounds to represent recurring usage and cross the promotion gate of 3.
tradeoffs: Two known frictions surfaced (recorded as constraints): cambium's lexical recall missed con-016 for the phrase 'documentation sync rule' (endorsement path used instead of recall), and parallel org PRs conflicted on knowledge.json.

summary: Normalize cp1252 mojibake at the write chokepoint (_new_item), not only in the KNOWLEDGE.md export renderer, so the canonical knowledge.json and recall() serve clean text.
problem: _demojibake ran only in the markdown export path, so distill/import/capture stored substrate content verbatim. A cp1252-mangled em-dash from a source (context-keeper .context/, an import export, an agentsync note) persisted in knowledge.json; recall() reads the store directly, so agents received the mojibake while the exported KNOWLEDGE.md looked clean. Observed live: two org items in jarmstrong158/knowledge carried a broken em-dash in the store while the .md rendered fine.
why_chosen: _new_item is the single normalize-and-write chokepoint every source already routes through (distill agentsync + context-keeper readers, import adapters, manual capture, valid_while premise), so one edit cleans content/why/tags/source.ref for all paths. _demojibake is already guarded (re-decodes only on a tell-tale lead byte AND a clean cp1252->utf-8 round-trip), so correct text and legitimately-accented content are never corrupted. Export keeps its own _demojibake as belt-and-suspenders for pre-existing data.
what_we_tried: Export-only demojibake (the prior state), which left the canonical store and recall() output corrupted while masking the problem in the published doc.
tradeoffs: Repair happens at ingest, so items already corrupted in a store are not retroactively cleaned by this change alone (handled separately by a data fix / re-render). Normalization runs on every write, a negligible cost since the guard short-circuits on pure-ASCII text.
retrieval_hints: why does cambium clean mojibake on write

summary: Gate team->org promotion on generalization: a project-specific body is refused at the org boundary unless restated via org_content= (kept concrete as `example`) or forced. Mirrors the endorsement gate; recall surfaces endorsed_as; review_promotions self-reports org_needs_generalization.
problem: promote(to_scope='org') was a pure scope-flag flip that copied the body verbatim. Scopes differ by readership (local=you, org=every project), so a single-repo runbody (names dashboard.py, clark_foundation.pt) promoted unchanged is useless or misleading org-wide, yet gets recalled/cited/institutionalized. The generalization already existed in the endorsement note but recall() buried it in the trust blob, so the right statement was captured and hidden while the wrong one was served.
why_chosen: Follows cambium's existing split (Decision 3): crossing into org is a hard gate, drift within a scope is a soft signal. This is a crossing, so it gets a gate mirroring the endorsement gate exactly (same not_* refusal, same force=True override). cambium stays deterministic/model-free: a regex lint (_org_body_smells_local: origin-project name, filenames, test ids, dec/con refs) detects the smell and hands back the endorsement note as a ready draft; the human restates. org_content becomes the org body and the concrete is preserved as example. Companion moves close the loop: recall surfaces endorsement notes as endorsed_as, and review_promotions reports org_needs_generalization so the tool self-diagnoses runbooks that crossed before the gate. Verified against the live org corpus: flags exactly the 4 project-specific items (plus one that names its own project) and leaves the 4 genuinely-universal ones clean.
what_we_tried: Considered warn-only lint (rejected: contradicts 'don't make the unsafe org path easy') and recall-only surfacing of endorsement notes (rejected as primary: treats the read symptom, leaves promote shipping un-generalized bodies) -- the latter folded in as a supporting move.
tradeoffs: The lint is precision-biased, not exhaustive: a bare word/word 'path' is deliberately NOT a tell (prose uses slashes for lists like survey/claim/update_status, and real paths of concern end in a filename the file-tell catches), so a bare directory path with no filename/project-name can slip through. Accepted because a false block is costlier than a false pass here, and force=True plus the review_promotions backstop catch the remainder.
retrieval_hints: why does promote to org require org_content
project-specific body refused at org boundary

summary: Add generalize() to remediate org items that crossed before the gate (restate body in place from the endorsement note, keep concrete as example, PR-batched); and make distill fall back why_chosen -> rationale for pre-v0.4 context-keeper decisions.
problem: The generalization gate (dec-003) only prevents FUTURE mis-scoped org crossings; the items already in org before it (5 in the live corpus) still had project-specific bodies with no tool-managed way to fix them — hand-editing knowledge.json was the anti-pattern the user rejected. Separately, the stack integration audit found distill read decision rationale from why_chosen only; context-keeper renamed rationale->why_chosen at v0.4 but still reads both, so a pre-v0.4 decision's entire WHY was silently dropped while its summary was kept.
why_chosen: generalize() is the remediation counterpart of the gate and reuses its parts: it edits a promoted item in place (org via a new _org_mutate_direct/_org_mutate_pr CAS path, team via _team_mutate), defaulting org_content to the item's latest endorsement note (where the restatement already lives), preserving the concrete body as example, and clearing the org_needs_generalization flag; PR mode batches all edits onto one shared cambium/generalize branch so remediation is one reviewable PR. Verified end-to-end: the 5 flagged live items generalized into knowledge#11, detector then clean. The rationale fallback (e.get(why_f) or e.get(\"rationale\")) mirrors context-keeper's own back-compat, one line, no downside.
retrieval_hints: how to fix an org item that is too project-specific
distill drops rationale for legacy decisions

summary: Make cambium project-aware for the solo-operator-is-org model: CAMBIUM_REPO defaults to the cwd git root when unset, so one config (org_repo + agent_id) serves every project; the SessionEnd hook also reads the session cwd from its payload.
problem: The operator is the org and works across many solo projects; local->team->org collapses to per-project->one shared knowledge brain. A global SessionEnd distill hook pinned to a single CAMBIUM_REPO would only ever capture one project, and interactive recall/promote would target the wrong repo.
why_chosen: Rather than require per-project cambium config, CAMBIUM_REPO falls back to the git root at/above cwd (new _git_root helper) in _cfg and _config_state when set in neither env nor config file. One config sets the stable pieces (CAMBIUM_ORG_REPO = the knowledge repo, CAMBIUM_AGENT_ID); the project is whatever repo the session is in. _config_source reports source 'cwd' so status stays coherent. The SessionEnd hook additionally reads the payload cwd and sets CAMBIUM_REPO (belt-and-suspenders, since the hook process cwd may differ from the session project). Team scope is vestigial for a solo org but harmless. Verified: cambium 63/63, xylem 75/75.
retrieval_hints: cambium defaults CAMBIUM_REPO to current project
solo dev is the org config

summary: Do not add semantic (embedding) recall to cambium yet. Keep the lexical token-overlap scorer (_score). Re-evaluate when the knowledge base crosses ~100 items using bench_recall.py.
problem: Recall is currently lexical (token overlap), documented in _score as "the semantic upgrade is a later swap." Raised: how hard is semantic recall and is it worth doing now? Needed a data-backed answer before spending effort, and before giving up the zero-dependency property that embeddings would cost.
why_chosen: Ran bench_recall.py against the live 9-item knowledge base with 18 queries (9 control sharing vocabulary, 9 paraphrases using different words). The correct item scored 0.50-1.00 in ALL 18 cases, always far above the 0.2 abstention floor; zero true recall misses. Paraphrase top-1 (89%) equaled control (89%): substring matching plus double-weighted tags close the synonym gap at this scale. The only 2 top-1 failures were the same homonym collision on "sync" (decision-log-sync vs state-mirror-sync), which embeddings do NOT reliably fix and which surface at rank 2 anyway (limit=5). Embeddings would cost a dependency, kill the zero-dependency selling point, force recalibration of the floor, and fix nothing the data shows.
what_we_tried: Built bench_recall.py (in cambium repo) as the repeatable measurement. Considered three embedding approaches: local model (fastembed/ONNX MiniLM ~90MB), embedding API (Voyage/OpenAI; Claude has no embeddings endpoint), and hybrid flag-gated reranking. Hybrid is the preferred approach IF the trigger fires.
tradeoffs: Homonym near-misses (correct item at rank 2) persist for now; mitigated by tag hygiene, not embeddings. Result is sample-size-limited: 9 items is tiny and tag-rich, so the picture changes with more or sloppier entries, hence the explicit re-eval trigger.
constraints_created: Re-run bench_recall.py when the cambium knowledge base crosses ~100 items; build hybrid embedding recall only if paraphrase top-1 drops below ~70% OR a gold item falls below the abstention floor. Homonym collisions alone are not a trigger; fix tags instead.
retrieval_hints: should we add semantic recall to cambium

summary: Added solo/team auto-detection to cambium with a solo fast-lane for org promotion. Detection is automatic and reversible; the generalization gate is never relaxed.
problem: cambium's promotion ladder (local -> team -> org) rests on three trust signals, two of which assume other people: peer endorsement and org PR review. For a solo builder those collapse to self-endorsement and self-reviewed PRs, so the team tier and the mandatory endorsement gate become ceremony with no one to perform them. The user wanted the process to be effective solo WITHOUT removing any team utility.
why_chosen: Detection is inferred, not configured: _detect_mode() counts distinct identities ever seen (configured agent + agentsync claim owners + endorsement by-fields across local/team/org); <=1 is solo. CAMBIUM_MODE=auto|solo|team overrides. In solo mode, promote(item_id, to_scope="org") may originate from LOCAL (skips the team hop) and the deliberate promote call itself counts as the endorsement (auto-stamped {by: agent} for the audit trail). This is purely additive: team mode behavior is byte-identical to before, so nothing is lost when a team exists. It is reversible with zero migration: the day a second collaborator claims or endorses anything, detection flips to team and the full peer-endorsement ladder re-engages. The user chose the "fast lane" option (promote = the vouch) over keeping a separate explicit endorse step.
what_we_tried: Considered a plain CAMBIUM_SOLO flag (rejected: manual, and would collapse the tier rather than adapt). Considered auto-satisfying endorsement in solo mode without allowing the direct local->org hop (that was the runner-up option offered). Chose inference + fast-lane because it grows with the user.
tradeoffs: The one quality bar that is NEVER relaxed is the generalization gate (_org_body_smells_local): a project-specific body still must be restated via org_content before it can reach org, in every mode. So solo speeds up the ceremony but not the abstraction discipline, which is the piece that actually matters for a multi-project solo dev. Cost: _detect_mode does a git fetch of the agentsync branch (acceptable, promote already does git ops).
constraints_created: The solo fast-lane must never relax cambium's generalization gate (_org_body_smells_local): a project-specific body always requires an org_content restatement (or force=True) before promotion to org, regardless of solo/team mode.
retrieval_hints: how does cambium detect solo vs team
why can I promote local straight to org
